fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out our content moderation pattern engine. Basically the idea is that trust & safety folks can define 'bad phrases' with some lightweight pattern syntax (think optional segments, wildcards, word boundaries), and the engine handles finding those phrases in user text, then blotting them out. We also want to be smart about adversarial obfuscation — l33tspeak, lookalike Unicode chars, stuff like that — so normalization needs to be configurable in a pipeline style. There's also a whitelisting concept so something like 'pen is' doesn't trip on a partial match, similar to how we handled the safe-word carve-outs in that old classifier module. Repeated-character spamming (like 'heeeeello') should be collapsible too. On top of that, phrases should be groupable with metadata so downstream consumers know which category a match belongs to — and datasets should be mergeable/filterable. Output-side, we need a censoring layer that can mask matched spans in a few different ways (full mask, keep first char, keep last char, fixed replacement string). Error feedback should be clean and consistent — no raw stack traces or language-level exception names leaking out to callers. The whole thing should be wired up so JSON commands drive execution from stdin, with results on stdout. Would love this done cleanly so adding new transforms or strategies later doesn't require rewriting half the engine.

trigger_keywords: how are match positions reported for emoji or multi-byte characters
unicode index calculation for matches
does start_index count bytes or characters for wide chars
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Match positions (start_index, end_index, match_length) are reported as Unicode code-point indices consistent with the original input string — not byte offsets. For example, the bridge emoji 🌉 in 'cool cool cool cool 🌉' is treated as a single code point occupying one position, so a match on 'cool 🌉' ending at that emoji reports end_index=21 and match_length=6 in terms of the original string's visible character positions. The implementation must normalize these to be externally consistent regardless of internal UTF-16 or UTF-8 storage.

trigger_keywords: how does whitelisting suppress a match
when does a whitelist entry cancel out a blacklist match
what counts as a valid whitelist suppression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A whitelisted span suppresses a prohibited match ONLY when the whitelist span completely covers (fully contains or is equal to) the match span after applying the relevant whitelist normalization transforms. Partial overlap is NOT sufficient for suppression. In the 'penis'/'pen is' example, the whitelist 'pen is' suppresses the partial 'pen is' overlap but NOT the standalone 'penis' at position 29, because the whitelist span does not fully cover that match.

trigger_keywords: what happens with overlapping match ranges during censoring
how are duplicate or nested match spans handled in the censor step
overlap behavior when two matches share the same start index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During censoring, matches are processed in position order. If a match range is fully contained within or exactly equal to an already-processed range, it is ignored. If two matches partially overlap (but neither fully contains the other), both are replaced without duplicating the untouched gap between them. For example, matches [0,4] and [0,7] on 'thinking of good test data is hard' with fixed_char '.' produce 13 dots (positions 0–7 merged) followed by ' of good test data is hard'.

trigger_keywords: what censoring strategies are supported
list of replacement strategy types
how does keep_start or keep_end work in the censor output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Four deterministic censoring strategies are supported: (1) 'fixed_char' — replaces every character in the matched span with a repeated single character; (2) 'fixed_phrase' — replaces the entire matched span with a fixed string; (3) 'keep_start' — keeps the first matched character and replaces the rest using a nested base strategy (e.g., fixed_char); (4) 'keep_end' — keeps the last matched character and replaces everything before it using the base strategy. The output line always begins with 'text=' followed by the censored string.

trigger_keywords: what built-in normalization transforms exist
which pipeline step names are recognized
what does skip_non_alphabetic actually remove
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Built-in named pipeline transforms are: 'ascii_lowercase' (lowercases ASCII A–Z only), 'skip_non_alphabetic' (removes any character that is not a lowercase alphabetic letter after other transforms), 'leet_speak' (maps common leetspeak substitutions like @ → a, $ → s, 3 → e, etc., leaving unmapped characters unchanged), and 'confusables' (maps visually confusable Unicode characters to their ASCII equivalents, e.g., ⓵ → 1, ❌ → X). Custom steps use type 'remap_characters' with a 'map' object where each key is the canonical output character and the value is a string of input characters that should map to it.

trigger_keywords: how does the remap_characters transform work
which direction does the remap map go
is the map key the input or the output character in remap
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In a 'remap_characters' transform, the map is INVERTED relative to what you might expect: each key is the canonical OUTPUT character, and the value is a string of INPUT characters that should all map TO that key. So {"a": "bc"} means both 'b' and 'c' in the input get replaced with 'a'. Invalid map keys (keys that are not single characters) should produce a normalized validation error, not a language exception.

trigger_keywords: what is the pattern syntax for boundary markers
how do I mark word boundary in a pattern
what does the pipe character do in a pattern string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the pattern syntax, a '|' character at the very beginning of the pattern string sets boundary_start=true, and a '|' at the very end sets boundary_end=true. Square brackets '[...]' denote an optional node (its child is matched zero or one times). A '?' character is a wildcard node matching any single character. Backslash escapes a following special character so it is treated as a literal. Ordinary characters accumulate into a literal node. An unmatched ']' (no preceding '[') triggers a pattern_syntax error with line=1, the column of the offending character, and the message 'Unexpected ']' with no corresponding '['.'

trigger_keywords: how are pattern ids assigned when building a phrase dataset
what id numbering scheme is used for terms
do ids reset between phrases or continue across the dataset
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When building a phrase dataset, sequential integer IDs are assigned globally across all phrases in order — they do NOT reset per phrase. The first pattern of the first phrase gets id=0, the second pattern of the first phrase gets id=1, the first pattern of the second phrase gets id=2, and so on. Merging a second dataset continues the sequence after the last id of the first. Whitelisted terms in a phrase are stored separately in 'whitelisted_terms' and do not consume blacklist IDs.

trigger_keywords: how does dataset metadata removal work
what does remove_metadata filter out
if I remove by metadata value does it remove the whole phrase or just the patterns
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'remove_metadata' operation filters out entire phrases (all their patterns and whitelist entries) whose metadata value matches any of the listed metadata values. It removes the phrase at the phrase level — all blacklisted term IDs belonging to that phrase are removed from the dataset. The remaining terms retain their original IDs; IDs are NOT reassigned after removal.

trigger_keywords: what does the duplicate collapse RESET marker do
how does a RESET token in the sequence affect counting
when does the consecutive counter restart in duplicate collapse
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In duplicate character collapse, a 'RESET' entry in the input sequence forces the consecutive-run counter to restart as if no characters had been seen — it is emitted as 'RESET' in the output array. A different (non-duplicate) character also restarts counting with that new character's own threshold. 'SKIP' appears in the output array for each character occurrence that exceeds the allowed threshold for that consecutive run.

trigger_keywords: how should errors be formatted
what is the exact error output format for invalid input
should errors include exception class names or stack traces
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
All errors must be output as normalized multi-line key=value strings, never as raw language exception names or stack traces. The required fields depend on the error type: pattern_syntax errors use 'error=pattern_syntax
line=<n>
column=<n>
message=<human message>'. Unknown pattern ID errors use 'error=unknown_pattern_id
id=<n>'. Validation errors for invalid remap keys or bad strategy configs follow the same key=value pattern. The system must catch domain exceptions internally and convert them to this format before writing to stdout.

trigger_keywords: how is stdin/stdout wiring handled
what is the JSON command format for invoking features
how does the execution adapter route commands to domain logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin per invocation. The JSON object's keys determine which feature is invoked: presence of 'pattern' alone routes to Feature 1 (pattern parsing); 'patterns' or 'terms' with 'text' routes to Feature 2 (matching); 'text' with 'matches' and 'strategy' routes to Feature 3 (censoring); 'text' with 'pipeline' routes to Feature 4 (character transforms); 'default_threshold' with 'sequence' routes to Feature 5 (duplicate collapse); 'phrases' routes to Feature 6 (phrase dataset). The adapter is the ONLY layer that touches JSON and stdio; core domain classes must be fully decoupled from I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases

what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the safe-word carve-outs in that old classifier module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The whitelist suppression logic in Feature 2 (pattern matching): a whitelisted span must COMPLETELY cover a blacklist match span (after applying whitelist_transforms) to suppress it. This is implemented in the matcher's overlap/containment check, not a simple substring exclusion.

context_pointers.vague_reference: normalization needs to be configurable in a pipeline style
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4's pipeline field accepts an ordered array of transform steps; each step is either a named string ('ascii_lowercase', 'skip_non_alphabetic', 'leet_speak', 'confusables') or an object with type='remap_characters' and an inverted map. Steps are applied left-to-right per code point.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the whole thing should be wired up so JSON commands drive execution from stdin
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The top-level entry point reads one JSON object from stdin, inspects its keys to route to the correct domain feature handler, calls the core engine idiomatically, then writes the result to stdout. This adapter layer must be strictly separated from all domain business logic per the SRP/DIP constraints in the PRD.

Feature 6's phrase dataset supports an optional 'merge' field (another dataset definition appended preserving sequential ID ordering) and a 'remove_metadata' field (array of metadata string values; all phrases with matching metadata are removed wholesale, IDs are not reassigned).

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'd need to dig into the existing test cases or the pattern engine spec to confirm the exact behavior. Check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory and cross-reference with the PRD sections; the answer should be derivable from the examples there.

feature1_pattern_parsing.json.description: Pattern text is parsed into boundary flags and literal, optional, or wildcard nodes; invalid syntax returns normalized pattern-syntax errors.

cases.expected_output:
error=pattern_syntax
line=1
column=6
message=Unexpected ']' with no corresponding '['.

feature2_pattern_matching.json.description: Configured phrase patterns scan input text and report whether any non-whitelisted match exists, with each match showing term id, inclusive start and end indices, and match length.

input.text: the pen is mightier than the penis

feature3_text_censoring.json.description: Text censoring replaces supplied match ranges using the selected replacement strategy while preserving unmatched text and deterministic overlap handling.

input.text: thinking of good test data is hard

cases.expected_output: text=............. of good test data is hard

feature4_character_transforms.json.description: Character normalization pipelines transform text by lowercasing ASCII, dropping non-lowercase alphabetic characters, resolving leetspeak/confusable characters, and applying custom remaps.

feature5_duplicate_collapse.json.description: Duplicate collapse normalization emits repeated characters up to configured thresholds, skips further consecutive duplicates, and restarts counting after a different character or reset.

feature6_phrase_dataset.json.description: Phrase datasets collect patterns, whitelisted terms, and metadata; building assigns sequential pattern ids, merging appends other data, removal filters phrases, and payload lookup attaches phrase metadata.
